content.text: Fitness Functions for Continuous Design Validation

A fitness function is an automated check that verifies a specific architectural invariant holds after every change to the codebase. The term originates in evolutionary computation, where a fitness function measures how well a candidate solution satisfies the design objectives. In software architecture, Neal Ford and Rebecca Parsons popularised the concept in Building Evolutionary Architectures: a fitness function does not test that a particular function returns the right value, it tests that a structural or behavioural property of the entire system remains intact — that the architecture continues to satisfy its own design intent as the code evolves.

Claude integrations benefit from fitness functions more than most software systems because they face two layers of evolution simultaneously: the application code evolves through normal feature development, and the model itself evolves through version updates that can change behaviour even when the application code is unchanged. Without fitness functions, architectural erosion in Claude integrations is invisible until a production incident surfaces it — a prompt string assembled inline rather than through the prompt builder, a Claude call that bypassed the retry handler, a response parser that started extracting JSON fields directly instead of going through the typed response model. These violations accumulate silently until the system becomes brittle.

attrs.alt: Fitness function lifecycle: Define -> Implement -> Run in CI -> Evolve, with Structural, Behavioural, and Operational categories

content.text: The lifecycle above applies to all three categories of fitness function relevant to Claude integrations. Structural fitness functions enforce call-site and layer-boundary invariants by analysing the source code's import graph or AST. Behavioural fitness functions verify that output quality invariants hold across a representative eval set. Operational fitness functions verify that latency, cost, and token-budget constraints are not violated by architectural changes. Each category answers a different question: structural asks whether the code is organised the way the team decided, behavioural asks whether the system produces the quality it promised, and operational asks whether the system performs within the bounds the team committed to.
Structural Fitness Functions

Structural fitness functions inspect the shape of the source code — imports, call sites, and class hierarchies — rather than executing the application at runtime. They are cheap to run (milliseconds, no network calls) and completely deterministic, making them ideal for CI gates that block merges. Because they operate on static code, they catch architectural violations at the moment they are introduced rather than after they have propagated through the system.
Behavioural Fitness Functions

Behavioural fitness functions run a fixed eval set through the live Claude integration and assert that key quality metrics remain above defined thresholds. Unlike unit tests, behavioural fitness functions tolerate non-determinism: Claude will not produce identical outputs on every run, so the assertion is statistical (correctness rate across 50 golden inputs must be at least 92%) rather than exact (output must equal a specific expected string). The eval set is a maintained corpus of representative inputs with associated expected outputs or quality criteria.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Callable, List, Optional


@dataclass
class EvalCase:
    case_id: str
    input_text: str
    expected_keywords: List[str]    # All must appear in output
    forbidden_keywords: List[str]   # None may appear in output
    max_output_words: Optional[int] = None

    def evaluate(self, actual_output: str) -> "EvalResult":
        present = [kw for kw in self.expected_keywords if kw.lower() in actual_output.lower()]
        forbidden = [kw for kw in self.forbidden_keywords if kw.lower() in actual_output.lower()]
        word_count = len(actual_output.split())
        length_ok = self.max_output_words is None or word_count <= self.max_output_words
        passed = (
            len(present) == len(self.expected_keywords)
            and len(forbidden) == 0
            and length_ok
        )
        return EvalResult(
            case_id=self.case_id,
            passed=passed,
            missing_keywords=list(set(self.expected_keywords) - set(present)),
            forbidden_found=forbidden,
            word_count=word_count,
        )


@dataclass
class EvalResult:
    case_id: str
    passed: bool
    missing_keywords: List[str]
    forbidden_found: List[str]
    word_count: int


@dataclass
class BehaviouralFitnessFunction:
    function_id: str
    name: str
    eval_set: List[EvalCase]
    pass_threshold: float               # e.g. 0.92 = 92% of cases must pass
    invoke_fn: Callable[[str], str]     # calls the Claude integration under test

    def run(self) -> "BehaviouralFitnessResult":
        results = []
        for case in self.eval_set:
            actual = self.invoke_fn(case.input_text)
            results.append(case.evaluate(actual))
        pass_rate = sum(1 for r in results if r.passed) / len(results)
        return BehaviouralFitnessResult(
            function_id=self.function_id,
            pass_rate=pass_rate,
            threshold=self.pass_threshold,
            results=results,
        )


@dataclass
class BehaviouralFitnessResult:
    function_id: str
    pass_rate: float
    threshold: float
    results: List[EvalResult]

    def passed(self) -> bool:
        return self.pass_rate >= self.threshold

    def summary(self) -> str:
        status = "PASS" if self.passed() else "FAIL"
        failing = [r.case_id for r in self.results if not r.passed]
        return (
            f"[{self.function_id}] {status}: "
            f"{self.pass_rate:.1%} pass rate (threshold {self.threshold:.1%})\n"
            f"Failing cases: {failing if failing else 'none'}"
        )
Operational Fitness Functions

Operational fitness functions assert that the system's non-functional properties — latency, token cost, and API error rate — stay within bounds after a change. These functions are especially important in Claude integrations because architectural changes that look innocuous (adding a parsing step, switching to a longer system prompt, enabling streaming) can materially affect latency or cost in ways that only become apparent under realistic traffic patterns.
Fitness Function Governance

Fitness functions need governance in the same way that test suites need governance: they accumulate, become stale, and must be updated when the architecture intentionally changes. A fitness function that checks an invariant the team no longer intends to enforce is worse than no fitness function — it either gets disabled (becoming dead code) or forces artificial code structures to satisfy a check that no longer represents real design intent.
The following table captures the governance practices that keep a fitness function suite healthy over the life of a Claude integration project:
Governance Practice
Cadence
Responsible
Output
New FF authored alongside every ADR
Per decision
Decision author
FF linked to ADR in both documents
CI failure triage — fix or retire FF
Per violation
PR author
Fix code, tighten FF, or file ADR to retire
Quarterly FF audit
Quarterly
Tech lead
Stale FFs retired; coverage gaps identified
Model version upgrade FF review
Per model upgrade
Platform engineer
Behavioural FF thresholds recalibrated
FF coverage report in architecture review
Per review
Architecture board
Open risks where invariants lack automated checks
Implementing a Fitness Function Registry

A registry makes the suite of active fitness functions visible across the team. Instead of discovering what invariants the system enforces by reading CI configuration files, developers can query the registry to see which properties are currently enforced, who owns each one, and whether any have recently been suppressed or overridden. The registry also enables the quarterly audit: the tech lead runs the registry report rather than grepping CI files.
Summary

Fitness functions give Claude integration teams a continuous, automated assertion that the architecture remains internally consistent as both the application code and the underlying model evolve. Structural fitness functions catch call-site and layer-boundary violations at merge time using static analysis. Behavioural fitness functions verify quality invariants hold across a representative eval set after every prompt or model change. Operational fitness functions alert when latency, cost, or error-rate targets are breached by architectural changes. Together, the three categories provide comprehensive coverage: the architecture is correctly structured, behaviourally sound, and operationally within bounds.
Pair every Architecture Decision Record with at least one fitness function that verifies the decision's core invariant is enforced by the codebase.
Run structural fitness functions in CI on every PR — they are cheap, deterministic, and catch violations at the point of introduction.
Run behavioural fitness functions against a stable eval set; assert a pass rate rather than exact output to tolerate model non-determinism.
Recalibrate operational fitness function thresholds after every intentional architectural change, not just after failures.
Maintain a fitness function registry and audit it quarterly; retire stale functions and identify coverage gaps before they become production incidents.
